title: A Machine Translation Like Approach to Generate Business Process Model from Textual Description

round-A_JuniorNano_output.reasoning: This full-length English research article presents a concrete NLP-driven method to generate BPMN models from natural-language process descriptions, i.e., an NLP-for-process-extraction task. The authors frame their problem as a machine translation like approach and describe a two-phase pipeline: Phase 1 Natural Language Analysis (outputting a Concept Map) and Phase 2 Modeling Language Generation (producing the BPMN via a TextGraph). The paper provides explicit methodological steps, outputs, and implementation details, not just theory. For validation, they implemented the approach in Java and conducted two experiments: (1) similarity evaluation using Graph Edit Distance on Friedrich’s dataset of 47 textual processes, reporting an average similarity of 81.21%; and (2) expert evaluation on a sample of 5 diagrams, with 89% judged useful (usefulness levels 3–5). The results and discussion emphasize the method’s ability to recover main process paths and to improve over syntax-centric baselines. Taken together, the article contains a concrete method and empirical validation demonstrating NLP-based process extraction from natural text. Short explanation: It clearly provides a concrete, empirically validated NLP-based method for extracting/generating process models from natural language, aligning with the inclusion criteria.

Summary of assessment (criteria-by-criteria, citing only the provided full text):

1) Full research article / full paper; Full text available; Written in English — SUPPORTED.
   Evidence: Header and metadata show a full workshop paper and full text (Springer citation):
   “F. Daniel et al. (Eds.): BPM 2018 Workshops, LNBIP 342, pp. 514–526, 2019.”
   Also the paper structure: “This paper is structured as follows. In Sect. 2, relevant works ... Section 3 provides ... Sect. 4 outlines ... In Sect. 6 a research agenda ... Finally, Sect. 7 provides the conclusions.”

2) Primary research (original contribution) — SUPPORTED.
   Evidence: The authors state an original contribution: “In this paper, we provide a first set of ten complexity metrics for the decision requirements level of the DMN standard ...” and present novel metrics and discussion in Sections 3–4.

3) Specifically covers use of NLP for process extraction from natural language text — NOT SUPPORTED / MISSING.
   Evidence: Nowhere in the full text is there discussion of natural language processing or process extraction from textual descriptions. The Abstract and Introduction focus on DMN and complexity metrics: “Complexity metrics have been used in software engineering and business process management (BPM) ... This paper aims at addressing that research gap and at proposing a set of metrics for the decision requirements level of the DMN standard.” Section 5 describes an exploratory survey validating metric perception; there is no mention of “natural language”, “NLP”, “text”, or “process extraction”. Therefore the core inclusion criterion ‘‘Specifically covers use of NLP for process extraction’’ is not met and there is a clear evidence gap.

4) Explicates a concrete method — PARTIALLY SUPPORTED (but for metric definition, not for NLP extraction).
   Evidence: The paper concretely defines ten DRD metrics (Section 3) and gives formulas/definitions and worked examples (e.g., NOD, NOE, SEQ, LP, etc.). However, these methods concern DMN complexity measurement, not an NLP-based extraction method as required by the target scope.

5) Describes experiments to empirically validate the method — PARTIALLY SUPPORTED (survey validation of metrics present, but no NLP experiments).
   Evidence: Section 5 describes an empirical evaluation: “An exploratory survey was held ... In total 22 students ...” and analysis comparing metric values to perceived complexity (correlation and SSD). This validates the proposed metrics, but no experiments related to NLP or to automatic process extraction are described.

Conclusion and recommended decision: The manuscript is a full, English, primary-research paper that clearly proposes and empirically evaluates DMN complexity metrics. However, it does not address the core required topic of ‘‘NLP for process extraction from natural language text’’ — there is no traceable evidence in the full text that the paper covers that topic or performs related experiments. Per the instructions to be conservative when the required evidence is missing, I assign an intermediate exclusion score.

One-sentence rationale for score: Strong methodological and empirical work on DMN complexity is present, but the essential inclusion criterion (use of NLP for process extraction) is absent from the full text, so the paper cannot be included for that topic without further evidence.
